title: With Orbán gone, Magyar reshapes Slovak-Hungarian dynamics and V4 balance
content:
For years, contentious issues in once highly tense relations between Bratislava and Budapest were carefully kept under wraps. Now, with the Fico-Orbán tandem collapsing, the suppressed disputes may resurface as political landmines, experts think.

On 12 April, Hungarians ended Viktor Orbán’s 16-year rule in a record turnout, choosing Péter Magyar as his successor.

After his Tisza party “swept” the electoral map, Magyar used his victory speech to promise a reset: restoring Hungary’s reputation in Europe, unlocking EU funds, and creating conditions that would encourage Hungarians abroad to return home.

He also pledged to strengthen cooperation within the Visegrád Group (V4). Yet, when outlining his first foreign visits, he placed Poland firmly at the top of the list, without mentioning Slovakia or Czechia at all.

Several signs suggest that relations with Slovakia could at least cool. During the campaign, Magyar sharply criticized Slovakia’s amendment related to “the Beneš decrees” (1945), and has since reiterated that he intends to
snippet: For years, contentious issues in once highly tense relations between Bratislava and Budapest were carefully kept under wraps. Now, with the Fico-Orbán tandem collapsing, the suppressed disputes may re
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: ETS Under Pressure as EU States Push Back
content:
Ten EU countries have rebelled against carbon rules they see as a threat to their domestic industrial sectors. At the center of the dispute is the Emissions Trading System (ETS).

The European Union’s Emissions Trading System (ETS) has been in place for over two decades and is widely regarded as one of the bloc’s most effective tools for reducing emissions. This assessment, however, is far from uniform, as the system’s effectiveness varies significantly across sectors.

In the power sector, the transition has been relatively dynamic, with the industry long integrated into the system and responsive to carbon pricing signals. A very different picture emerges in transport and buildings — two sectors where emissions remain persistently high and national decarbonisation strategies continue to fall short.

“Member states have not presented viable solutions to incentivise citizens and businesses to electrify and decarbonise transport — including public transit and cargo — or to reduce emissions from fossil fuel use in buildings,” said Kuba Gogolewski, programme director at Mission Possible.

It is precisely

creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Free EU train passes: How to secure the journey of a lifetime
content:
Free train travel across the entire European Union is quite literally within reach for thousands of young people stepping into adulthood. It’s not just a chance for the trip of a lifetime, but also an initiative aimed at fostering civic engagement and countering Euroscepticism

As part of the 40th anniversary of the Schengen Area, the European Union is investing in a project that only superficially resembles free holidays for eighteen-year-olds. In reality, it serves as a powerful tool for shaping civic attitudes, countering Euroscepticism, and reviving a centuries-old European educational tradition. Time is running out to secure a ticket in this year’s DiscoverEU edition.

For centuries, Europe cultivated a tradition known as the Grand Tour. Young people, on the threshold of adulthood, would set out on months-long journeys across the continent. The goal was simple: to discover new cultures, learn languages, engage with art, and above all, to find themselves and gain independence. Today, this classical model of entering adulthood is making a comeback in a democratized, environmentally friendly form under the DiscoverEU initiative, a f

creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: A Pro-Russian Turn or Systemic Change? Bulgaria Faces a Defining Choice
content:
The parliamentary elections in Bulgaria delivered a result whose scale surprised even the most seasoned observers of the political scene in Sofia.

Rumen Radev and his movement Progressive Bulgaria secured a parliamentary majority, signaling the end of the long-standing dominance of the centre-right GERB party led by Boyko Borisov.

“Radev’s victory was widely expected, but not on this scale. This marks the collapse of the old system centred around GERB, but not necessarily a systemic transformation for Bulgaria,” explained Dimitar Keranov of the German Marshall Fund.

Speaking to his supporters after the exit polls were announced, Radev himself left little doubt as to how he viewed the significance of the moment.
“Progressive Bulgaria has won decisively. This is a victory of hope over mistrust. A victory of freedom over fear,” he declared.

The defeat of Boyko Borisov and his GERB party, which had dominated Bulgarian politics for years, is one of the defining features of this election night. The former prime minister, casting his vote in Sofia, reacted to the out

creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Transport Remains the EU’s Biggest Climate Challenge
content:
A new report by Transport & Environment, State of European Transport 2026, forces Europe to take a hard look at itself: transport emissions are not falling, aviation is slipping out of control, and China dominates the global battery supply chain. 

At the same time, the data shows that the EU is just three years behind Beijing in the race for leadership in the electric vehicle market.

The Transport & Environment (T&E) has published its annual report on the state of European transport — and its message is clear: transport remains the EU’s largest source of greenhouse gas emissions and the only sector where emissions have increased since 1990 rather than declined.

While sectors such as energy, industry, construction and agriculture have reduced their carbon footprint, transport — including international aviation and shipping — continues to follow its own trajectory, largely resistant to successive climate policies.

creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Foreign Politicians Gather in Budapest: Orbán’s Pre-Election Message to Hungarian Voters
content:
Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orbán, who is running for re-election, and his Fidesz party use international events not only to strengthen ties with like-minded allies but also to send messages to a domestic audience.

In this instance, the Conservative Political Action Conference (CPAC) held in Budapest is intended to send a clear signal to voters: that the ruling party purportedly enjoys wide-ranging support not only at home but internationally as well. Members of the Nemunas Dawn faction, Karolis Neimantas and Dainoras Bradauskas, also took part in CPAC.

Opposition figures in the Seimas did not hold back in criticising their decision to attend, while political analysts also pointed to potential risks, warning that such a move could undermine Lithuania’s image as a staunch supporter of Ukraine. According to sources, the event centres on criticism towards the European Union, as well as globalisation and migration policy. The conference itself is closely tied to Orbán’s election campaign.

Meanwhile, the parliamentarians insist that their participation in the conference is in no way direct

creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Fertility crisis in Poland and Eastern Europe: Who will support ageing societies?
content:
Europe is facing a demographic crunch — marked by labour shortages, mounting pressure on pensions and healthcare, and the prospect of rising taxes. The reality is clear: Europeans are having fewer and fewer children.

According to the latest data from Eurostat, the number of births in Europe has been declining for yet another consecutive year, with the fertility rate falling well below the so-called replacement level of 2.1 children per woman. 

In practical terms, this means too few children are being born to replace the current generation of parents. Across the EU, fertility has now reached its lowest level since records began in 2001, standing at 1.34. Poland’s rate is even lower — at around 1.1 — placing it among the lowest in the bloc. For comparison, the lowest fertility rate is recorded in Malta (1.01), while Bulgaria reports the highest (1.7).

“We have gone through the demographic transition. Children are no longer needed to support households, they live longer, and more is invested in each of them. We no longer have child

creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Orbán, Magyar, and an Army of Bots. The Most Toxic Campaign in Hungary’s History
content:
The Hungarian election campaign has become a testing ground for AI and Russian disinformation. Just days before the most important vote in years, the country is gripped by an informational chaos that completely blurs the line between truth and falsehood.

The approaching April 12, 2026, is more to Hungarians than just another date on the electoral calendar. It is a moment when the country’s fate hangs in the balance, and for Viktor Orbán and his Fidesz party – the toughest test since taking power in 2010. A powerful rival has appeared on the horizon: Péter Magyar and his Tisza party. However, what was supposed to be a celebration of democracy has turned into a technological and disinformation nightmare.

Hungarian voters find themselves right in the middle of unprecedented chaos. They are bombarded from all sides by AI-generated fake tapes, espionage accusations, and massive attacks by Russian troll farms. Truth has become a highly scarce commodity in Hungary, and the election campaign has long since abandoned the rules of fair play.

Ukraine

title: Produce Less, Earn More: EU Debates Solutions to Milk Crisis
content:
Slovakia calls on the EU to pay farmers to produce less milk, but the European Commission has so far refused. 

Slovakia has officially requested in a letter that the European Commission provide financial assistance to dairy cattle farmers, who are facing increasing losses due to falling milk prices.

The appeal was also signed by Bulgaria, Hungary, and Italy. The letter was sent to the Commission ahead of last week’s EU Agriculture and Fisheries Council (AGRIFISH), where Slovak Agriculture Minister Richard Takáč (Smer-SD) also spoke about the critical situation.

“Our dairy farmers are clearly facing a crisis. I honestly do not understand why we are allowing this sector to become exhausted when we have tools to address the crisis,” he stated before the other agriculture ministers.

In its appeal, Slovakia warns that milk production at current prices and with rising energy and other production input costs is unprofitable and unsustainable in the long term.

creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Eastern Europe’s uneven front: How the East Flank nations stand on Ukraine
content:
Russia’s full-scale invasion of Ukraine in 2022 upended the security balance of Europe and forced its eastern neighbors to confront hard questions about their own safety, alliances, and political identity.

The policies of Eastern Flank countries toward Ukraine reveal a complex landscape characterized by both solidarity and divergence. This report examines the positions of nine countries across NATO and the EU’s eastern flank – Bulgaria, Czechia, Finland, Hungary, Latvia, Lithuania, Romania, and Slovakia – toward Ukraine. All these countries share a border of anxiety and a history marked by Moscow’s shadow. But their responses to the war have not been the same. Some, like Poland and the Baltic states, treat Ukraine’s survival as inseparable from their own. Others, notably Hungary and Slovakia, have balked at sending weapons and adopted a cautious, even contrarian tone.

Military and non-military support: from strategic commitment to cautious assistance

The frontline supporters

Poland, Lithuania, Latv
